top_rant.text:
Hello! I did not purchase these drums from Amazon. When doing my research on this kit I read and watched just about every review I could possibly take in. I read a lot of reviews that where useless. When reviewing a product you are reviewing the actual product not the service of the company you purchased it from. I am not going to go into a 10 page review either probably only 5 pages or so :). So with that venting being complete here we go!
First of I am guitar player since 1987 and I always wanted to learn drums. Bring 46 years old I figure now or never. I purchased a Simmons SD-500 to start, figuring “Hey, an entry level guitar now a days is just fine it must be the same with E-Drums”. WRONG! The SD-500 was one step up from Rock Band and basically more frustration then they were worth. So returned them and ordered the DM10x.

The Rack:
The rack is a VERY solid rack with chrome and hard plastic hardware. It is very stable and very nice quality. The drum hardware is metal with rugged clamps. I was very happy with this construction. The drums do NOT move on the rack when in play like other companies. Once you set them they stay unlike other companies. Cough… Simmons!

The Drums:
The drums are plastic shells and molded hard plastic base. Don’t let this scare you, Roland uses plastic on their E-kits from the TD-11 to the TD-30KV Pro. This is common in the E-Drum industry. They are good heavy quality construction. They are very slick looking. The drum sizes are SOOOOO much better than the rubber pads you get from other companies. Being a beginner I found myself actually not missing the drums from time to time.

I read review after review that how loud these drums where I expected them to be so terrible that he noise gods would immediately put me in prison for playing them. Ok all you guys that are freaking out about this. Get yourselves under control! Yes they are louder than mesh heads granted. But they are not that loud. The “Real Drum” heads have good bounce but are not as loud as everyone said. You can hear them bounce off the piezo plate in the drum. You can do mesh head conversions on these drums with very little cost. Do you need to race out to do it? That is all up to you. Try them out first and see how you like them. If you’re in an apartment complex and you can make no noise at all? Then do it. I did do the mesh head conversion. You can find out tons of info on that on [...] in the DIY section. A very friendly forum with an amazing amount of info.

But you just said they are not that loud why did you do that? Well… I kind fell into the hype and complaints. The mesh head conversion is easy and cheap and it did make them quieter and reworking the foam did make them feel better. Getting the resonance plate lower in the foam did help the feel of the drum. That I admit. But right out of the box these where a quality product and hell you might like the Mylar heads. Some people love them.
The drums have great rebound with the mesh heads and are very close to an acoustic feel. Exactly the same? No… How do I know that being a beginner? Well I have a pearl export acoustic kit that I cannot play in my duplex and keep my tenants. But I did test bounce after doing the conversion side by side (for real) with a tuned acoustic set. I tested the number of bounces and how an acoustic kit responds sound wise. And these drums with the mesh heads converted are pretty sweet.

The adjustments on these drums are great! You have sensitivity, cross talk (meaning do you trigger another drum on the rack or not.), retrigger, and many other setting. If you think you’re buying E-drums and they are all set up you are wrong. You will eventually mess around with the setting. Hell that is a lot of the fun. Again the DMdrummer web site has tons of info on doing this. Just sit back and do some reading from people that are SERIOUS about E-Drums. But out of the box they do play well but you will make them how you want.

Are the drums sensitive? Yes! You can get the sizzle of a nice drum role. You can play quiet and rockin’ loud when you want to.

The Cymbals:
I read the cymbals are terrible and I would say they are lacking on initial set up. The choke is there on the crash 1 and ride. But not on the crash 2. You can find info on how to fix this and how to make them respond nicely. You also can tweak the module to make choking 3 cymbal and more realistic choke. So don’t open the kit and plug it in and say THESE SUCK! They don’t they need some tweaking. So does a acoustic set. They are NOT loud at all when hitting them. And they are quality and feel nice and heavy. Nice big sizes and they can be kept loose so they move nicely when you whack them! You’re going to look like Peter Chris or Jon Bonham in no time! :) Or at least you think you will like me! Beth I hear you calling, but I just can’t come home right now… Me and boys are play’n and we just can’t find the sound. Ok, ok! I will stop…

The Module:
The best on the market? No… Roland and Yamaha are said to have better modules. But if you really get into this you can purchase software like EzDrummer, Superior Drummer or Addictive Drums to use your computer / laptop for your module.
WHAT!?!?!? You say? Yes it is a midi device and you can route the info to your laptop and make these drums sound amazing. With a regular USB printer cable. (Not included) Are these programs better than a high end E-Drum set up? Yep… So wait a sec? If I spend 7000.00 dollars on a Roland high end kit will I just route it to my laptop anyway. Probably…

Granted I have never sat down behind a Roland TD30kv so I cannot say that for sure on that set but I do know that a lot of people end up going the laptop as the module and only use the E-Drum module as a Midi device. It’s kind of exciting really… And kind of sad that these companies don’t have their modules as good as a 99 dollar downloadable program. So many options out there! You can make real drums into E-Drums very easily. There is soooo much info out there how to make amazing DYI drum conversions that rival the high end Roland’s and Yamaha kits. I am in the process of converting a Pearl Export snare as we speak. YES! I am officially out of control!

Anyway… I am letting my excitement let me ramble. The module has great kits. Some are just foolish and as a rock / blues guy I will never use them. I would say right now I have 10 kits written down that I love. But I can see having some fun times messing around with the 100 kits pre-installed on it. You also can make your own kits with the 1000 drum sounds on the module. This will keep you busy for quite some time.
Let me tell you something. If you are techie type and good with computers these DM-xx kits are geek heaven. This drum kit is the tweakers heaven! You don’t need to be this type but you can do tons of stuff with this module. You can download a utility and change all the kits to great sounding kits. Again the DMDrummer forum walks you through kits you can change. People even post their kits for you to try. You can get kits like John Bonham (Led Zepplin) (Yes I am old and think John was god.) sound or Red Hot Chili peppers or many other bands. This is HUGE! This is NOT an option of the module but the community around these kits is huge! Yes you are hacking the module but it is safe and you can always go back to default. (Disclaimer: I am not at fault if you get confused or break something.)

Again… This module is kind of a standard out there for messing around with it. I love that!

The Instructions:
I don’t need no stinking instructions! I don’t use them for a Wal-Mart TV stands or Staples Desks I just look at the picture and put it together. Only use instructions if you’re in trouble. Sorry ladies I am a dude that’s how I think.
Ok… I used the 1 page rack instructions and got through just fine. Just google any problems you might run into. I didn’t run into any but don’t listen to “OMG the instructions are terrible the sky is falling”. You have google! You can find out anything about these drums you want to know. It frustrates me with reviews giving less stars because of this. You are using the instructions once… Once and then you will have it. The internet exists. You will be just fine. Youtube has so many videos on these drums it is insane.

Conclusion:
Buy them! They are amazing and will keep you busy for quite some time. You’re going to be shocked on what you get. Alesis is dominating this price point. Ok the price… 1499? AGAIN google them. They are on Amazon right now for 1009.00. I found them for 959 through Walmart.com and Guitar Center didn’t flinch matching that price. Just print it up and bring it to them. They will check on the internet and do it. No hassle they were great. They gave me a little razzing that I found them at Walmart.com by Music123 but they did it. So I got a double base petal from Tama too. HEY! I saved some cash why not! So basically for 1150 I got a sweet double bass pedal and the sweet kit. I did not pay 1500 dollars for these things. And I got some Remo Mesh heads too. They are nice! Tough too!
So if you are a 100 year acoustic drummer will you like them? I think you will enjoy them. But if you’re an acoustic snob that will never accept electronic drums? Then why are reading this at all? Move on and make a sound proof studio in your house and buy a back belt if you’re going to “play out”. You’re going to need it carrying those acoustic drums everywhere you go. I am too old for that and in my studio an acoustic set is so loud I would need miracle ear in a about 2 days because of hearing loss. And no I am not wearing ear plugs for 3 hours and getting “broccoli ear” from having foam in my ear. I will do electronic drums.

It is frustrating that the music industry accepted electric guitar but not E-Drums. Dylan has gone electric? That ring a bell? Too young to know what I am talking about? Google it… It changed the world. They don’t sound like acoustic guitar right? But without them the rock world be in a very different place. The music industry accepted electric keyboards too! They don’t sound exactly like a 20,000 dollar grand piano. But the world loves them. But E-Drums get so many negative reviews because they are not like acoustic. My acoustic guitar is very different than my Les Paul. And I want it that way. I never purchased the Les Paul thinking it was going to be an acoustic. It’s not… Its electric I can do tons of things with it. It’s endless the sounds you can get out of that with a nice pedal. E-Drums are EXACTLY the same thing…
Josh has gone electric! :) Hope you found this review useful and maybe even humorous in some spots.

Disclaimer: I do not take responsibility for miss spelled words, type-o’s or terrible grammar. I didn’t like school and it shows! Besides it’s the internet… Not “English Comp 101”. :)
top_rant.title: I don't wanna  work! I want to bang on me drums ALL DAY!

top_rant.text:
Bought this from another store on sale, but got 2.

Initial Setup and Impression:

At first I thought I got a dud speaker...as I only hooked one up to test it out and it sounded very bland (not like studio flat kind of bland, but actually bad). I had to test with the cables and connectors I had on hand, but realized that becuase I was using a standard 1/8" earphone cable with an adapter that that was likely the reason. So I waited for the next day's delivery of my actual balanced 1/4" cables to come in to test it for real. Not only that, but I kept hearing a hiss coming from the speakers no matter which outlet I plugged them into. There were quite a few complaints about the hiss, but when I plugged in the new cables, these things not only got louder and clearer, but also got rid of the hiss. I'm kind of thrown aback with how good these sound over my old set of speakers (junky Logitech computer crap). So, of course these will sound better than what I had, but with the features these things have, I could put them near a wall or corner and the bass is still very clear and as pronounced as I like. It was easier to tweak the lows/mids/highs through my hardware EQ than bother with anything else. This gives me the ability to have them sound like an amazing pair of gaming speakers or flat studio monitors whenever needed! Hookup and setup were a breeze...just be sure to get the appropriate cables.

The Sound:

The audio profile on these is crazy. I can be in another room, and I and another person noticed these sounded like they were in the room with us. So, do I believe the waveguide tech mentioned is more than just marketing fluff?...you bet. Are these worth the suggest price. I believe so, but if you can get them on sale, that's a win/win. Get the balanced cables that work with your equipment and you won't regret it. I paid $15 for a pair of cables and it was well worth it. The build quality is pretty good, but the front feels a little cheap with a plastic front, albeit tough...still plastic. I hate how these look (prefer the look of the Mackie MR524), but it's the sound I was after. I did a ton of research on various monitors and brands (didn't really care which...just wanted a good deal on a great set and that's what I got). These rated and compared very well against many top brands and comparable models (Yamaha HS5, Mackie MR524, KRK Rockit 5, and about 10 others I took a look into). Some songs naturally sounded better or worse on these, but overall the comparisons kept showing over and over how these just did an overall better job of great sound for the price. The bass is good. It doesn't appear to distort, even at high volume (I've only taken them up to about 45%, since they can get VERY LOUD, and I don't want to risk blowing them out). I read a review claiming that you could hear even where instruments were recorded in a room, assuming they were recorded in the same room together. I checked through various recordings to find that's the case. I could tell where something had been recorded in what would have been behind me. The waveguide does work, as far as I can tell and it works VERY well.

The Design:

These weigh about 10lbs each, so keep that in mind when buying a stand. I currently just have them on my desk and don't really notice any distortion (granted I can put these at 30% volume on my computer with the speakers set in the back at about 70% and they're LOUD). I'm planning on building my own shelf-stand for these to meet my specific needs. Alternatively, I may consider stands from Rockville instead. Are these things big? Yeah...kind of. I would say they're about 7 inches wide, and are tall, so I don't really want them on the desk unless I can get some shelf-space underneath. I love how they have a visible, yet muted white light to show they're on and they do not have any power-down standby. I just wish they looked better than they do. I also didn't realize they made a white version of these until after I bought them, so knowing that, I would have chosen the white ones. As I said before, the waveguide tech does appear to work very well. I love how I can adjust frequency and volume settings to suite my needs. This comes in especially handy in my case, because I have the right speaker behind a video monitor (since I don't have stands yet). I was able to adjust the bass freqency and volume in such a way that I could still here it very clearly with the left speaker.

Overall Impression:

I would buy these again. That's my take-away. They're pretty solidly built, the sound is very impressive (fills the room kind of sound), are plenty loud (if I'm watching videos late at night I can put them at 4% computer volume and they're clear and loud enough without being obnoxious). JBL definitely needs to work on the visual appeal of these, but if that's why you're buying studio monitors, then you'll likely be happier just going for some of the crappy sounding, but great looking ones. The funny thing is JBL wasn't a contender for me at all when I started this journey of finding a good set of quality monitors. It took about 5 or 6 contistant and fair reviews for me to realize this is what I wanted. These are my take-aways from those reviews.

- Different music sounds different on different monitors, especially depending on how that music was mixed, so one set might sound fine with a particular song than another. This means that unless you listen to a particular genre all the time and it's mixed generally the same, then you should pick speakers that work best for those. If you're like me and have varying requirements for your speakers, then get the ones that perform best across a set of songs.

- Comparable monitors had varying results:
- Mackies were tight and sounded pretty decent up against these, but these just sounded better overall.
- Yamahas (which were my first pick until I found these) just sounded too flat and didn't appear to have any real bass when you wanted it. That's great for studio monitors, but not ones that you want to use for pure entertainment from time to time.
- The KRKs were bass-heavy...sounded clear at low frequncies, but definitely worse on the mids and highs.
- Edifiers sounded more open, but the bass just wasn't there and lack of the room-filling waveguide the JBLs have.
- Klipsch sounded close, but the bass just wasn't as rich. Audio Engine sounded a little pushed back...just not as full as the JBLs.
- Presonus weren't bad actually, which surprised me, but they again lack the openness of the waveguide and the for the price, the JBLs just one it for me.
- AirPulse sounded pretty good in all except the lows, which weren't bad, but not as good as the JBLs, which just sounded fuller.
- Adam Audio speakers topped the JBLs for me actually, but the price was way too high comparatively.

I would describe these as somewhere between the KRKs and the Yamaha in that they can be entertaining to listen to or flat as you want. It helps to have an EQ with these or any speakers to adjust as you wish. Would I say the bass is worth the price? Yes, these top most in this price range. Would I say you need a subwoofer in addition? For me, they're more than adequate for most people, but if you really want to feel the thundering lows, then you're going to want a subwoofer, as well. This means for someone like me, I can get by with these for a long time until I can afford a proper sub, but I don't feel like I need a killer sub to compensate too much. I would recommend getting one with a flexible crossover feature, though.
top_rant.title: Inexpensive, High Quality, Better Than Most in This Price Range

top_rant.text:
I don't use Ableton. There, I said it. I still have hardware synths and a sampler. I guess that I'm old-school insomuch that I really like the feel of keys, strings, and (oddly) pads that respond to touch. I sold a MPC1000 a few months ago as it was its features redundant in the setup, it didn't have the ease of use or some of the things that I expected from such an expensive instrument. I've been wanting drum pads that were flexible enough to control some software synth stuff *and* be programmable so it could manage some of the hardware, too. The Maschine that seemed suitable was 5 bills, which was way too much, imho, for the use that it would get - and the smaller "mini" controllers seemed way too simple and cheaply made. When this was released it was originally like 350 or 400 - again, too much for what use it was to receive in my setup. When the price dropped, it was enough of a motivator for a try.

So, here's the down and dirty. The fit and finish are a cut above what was expected for the price. The 100 dollar controllers on the market were always a turn-off because they seemed that they were worth like 20. Cheap and plastic-eeey. This isn't the most solid gear around - it isn't precision cut or extruded aluminum, but it is well made enough that it doesn't creak in my hands - it actually feels sturdy. Out of the box, it was clean and unscuffed. There was no protector sheet of plastic over the screen which seems to be common these days, but it was, again, perfect out of the box. It came packed with the proper USB cable. I didn't measure the length, but it was certainly long enough to hook up to the computer and give me plenty of room to swivel around without dragging half of my extraneous crap onto the floor. It has the ability to be powered by a wall-wart. It takes a 6v 1a positive center adapter, but there was no indication of the size of the connector. I suppose that one of the "multi-tip" ones will suffice...but they look all wal-marty (and who likes to look like they just came from peopleofwalmart.com?).

The four knobs above the sliders are endless digital encoders. They aren't sloppy at all. The sliders have a nice solid feel to the action, although the actual plastic covers seem to be somewhat cheap looking. They stay attached just fine, however. All of the buttons are firm. They respond with a pretty solid click, so you definitely know when they're pressed. This may be a turn off to some, but that's pretty low on my list of things to worry about. All of the lights are bright and easy to see in my space, which is poorly lit at best. The screen is somewhat fickle to viewing angle, however. Not to jump ahead, but the lights on the software match the lights on the hardware as they flash. It makes me stare a little *too* long at the pretty flashy things...

Now, I bet someone out there is wondering about the pad action. I'd answer that, but my cat is in my face. .. give me a ute...ok, somewhat better. So, the pad action. It's pretty good. They don't require a huge hit to get the max response. Light touches get light responses very easily. They respond "easier" than the MPC pads. They react evenly when hit anywhere on the pad - as far as I can tell - you don't have to always hit the exact center to get a response and side hits generate predictable results. The pad cover is a single piece of rubber that sits underneath the aluminum face plate. It's obvious as when one pad is gently pushed on a side, all of the other pads move. Again, this really doesn't concern me. What my worry is, as with any instrument that uses a pad interface, is how many strikes am I going to get before the contacts become worn? Obviously, that's not a question that I can answer as I've had this for only a couple of weeks. I can say, however, that I like the action of this better than the MPC because they respond better to *my* style - which isn't to pound the living crap out of rubber pads. They respond very well to touch *and* pressure after the initial hit (aftertouch).

The step sequencer is fine. It works exactly as it should, and it's easy to figure out. The only complaint that I have is that the memory is very limited. It requires a lot of track bouncing. You might be able to record an entire song by using lots of loops and constantly adding variety to the same measures by soloing specific parts and manipulating the effects. Imho, you'll have to be bouncing a lot of step-sequenced tracks into your DAW. If I'm not mistaken, m-audio even has a video of peeps doing this very thing. It's a limitation if you are coming from an MPC and are expecting the same sequencing ability. Frankly, I wasn't looking for a sequencer at all - that's why I use a DAW. To me, the step sequencer is a nice little addition and it opens up a few avenues that I was willing to give up when I let go of the MPC.

This is a very programmable box. I was able to get it to control my soft synths immediately, although it won't work with some of my software as there are some products that don't have (as far as I know) the ability to accept midi via USB to control them unless I make them appear as VSTi hosted by my DAW. Other than those few very odd oddities, anything that I can plug into my DAW or run as a standalone has responded perfectly. It was so easy to program and use that it's turned a couple of my hardware synths into makeshift drum synths.

The software that it came with is good. It isn't spectacular, but it's good. AirDrum and Arsenal are really easy to use - unless computers terrify you, there's nothing that you can do "wrong" or "incorrectly" that will screw up the electrical grid on your side of town. The effects and the synth...it's called the, um, Hybrid 3 (I'm horrid with names, had to look it up) is good. The filters aren't on the order of a Waldorf or Virus, but remember that you are buying something that is marketed as a controller, not a synth with a controller. With that it mind, the controller is excellent, and the included Hybrid is pretty good, but not spectacular.

Arsenal is an excellent librarian - easy to use and find whatever you are looking for. I had it programmed to look in multiple places for VSTi's and it did so without fail, although it is a little quirky. It has only two spaces where you can specify a path to look for VST instruments. *However,* if you tell it to look in one path, then send it on it's merry way with a click of the "OK" button, you can come back and enter in a different path by writing over the first - and Arsenal remembers what it found in the first path and still looks in the second. Kind-of wackadoodle, but it works, so I'm not complaining.

The trigger finger was easy enough to use that I got my son to pick it up and start programming beats on the step sequencer, specifying different kits, adjusting each sample on the pads, manipulating the effects, and assigning controllers for different parameters - all within 10 minutes. He's *never* taken to any of my synths that quickly and had such a quick reward for his efforts that he wanted to stay and play until I made him give it up. That is the kind of ease that I was looking for for me - and given it's price point, flexibility, build quality, and general usefulness in my setup, it's a definite five star deal. If it were 400, that would have placed it in direct competition with NI's Maschine - and it would never have been bought by me. For 200, it is a *remarkable* deal considering all that is offered over the offerings at the 100 price point.

It's a keeper for me.
top_rant.title: Awesome controller!!

top_rant.text:
Edit: May 1, 2019
For those of you still complaining about the hi-hat trigger, search YouTube for the account Siren-Drums and check his video "Alesis Strike || How to dial in your Hi-Hat for MAXIMUM PLAYABILITY." Like with anything custom, dial in your settings yo. I'm not quite a year in, but I'm still loving this kit; and with as many video tutorials that are out there, I can't imagine that ever changing.

---

Full disclosure: I'm not a drummer. While I did play 20 years back in school and college, I've mostly forgotten what little coordination I had at the time. In the last few years I've had it in my head to slowly get back into so many instruments that I've left by the wayside. Drums being a primary goal. I have an acoustic kit and it sounds lovely; however, I do not! Being so self-conscious of my playing in a neighborhood environment, relearning has been difficult to say the least.

I've been eyeing the Strike Pro for about a year now comparing it to the DM10 and Roland equivalents. My thoughts were that if I could get a decent enough eKit to drown out the outside world, then maybe I can really get excited by playing again without pissing off the neighbors, and (mostly) without showcasing how bad I actually am to those around me. I have read review upon review upon review and in spite of the wave of issues that I see so many seem to have with it, I decided to go with the Strike.

Putting this kit together was intense! But once complete, it is as stable as a rock. I should note 2 things that I did not readily see at first. The Strike Pro kit comes with 3 "G" posts. I did not notice they two were taller than the third until halfway into assembly when I noticed that my stand was not at all working. So yes. Be mindful of that and place the two longer posts in the middle section next to each other around where the kick would sit. Seems obvious after the fact, but I just didn't catch it.

The second thing that I noticed was the kit is HUGE! Good news for those ordering the Pro version is that you will have two extra 24" horizontal mounting bars that are unused. I simply replaced the wider 36" bars with the two 24" bars and narrowed my footprint by a good 1.5'. As long as you mount your tom L clamps facing out, so that the toms hover above the frame bars, you can fit your two high toms on one 24" bar.

I use the other 24" on my left, behind my hi-hat. I then push the Module mount bar out beyond the kit frame rather than immediately next to the hi-Hat as most images show. I do this because it places the module more behind me to my left, and it gets it well away from the hi-hat and left crash where it would seem to be at risk of a missed hit. Perfect fit for me and it now fits in my corner so much nicer!

As mentioned, my goal was to use this kit to practice and learn. Since plugging my headphones in and actually playing? I have to say that I'm dumbfounded by how great this kit both feels and sounds. Closing my eyes I forget that I'm behind an eKit and instead imagine myself beating the hell out of a mic'd acoustic. It really is that good.

My module came with v1.2 OS installed and it sounded great, but there were some issues. Nothing major, but still enough to make me eager for the current v1.4 upgrade. Be aware of your screen brightness knob on the back of your module before the update! I prefer my screens fairly dim because I play in low light. The v1.4 update startup screen seems to automatically dim the screen by 25%. Oddly, I generally kept my screen by default around 25%. So after the update my screen appeared to go completely black. It took me days to figure this out and even a post to the Alesis support forums before someone nudged me reminding me that there is a dial for brightness. Yup. I'm an idiot. Still though, others were complaining about the same issue so please do not panic if this happens to you!

Having adjusted my screen BACK to a reasonable brightness that will not auto-dim to 0 on startup, I am set and happy. I've followed a few YouTube channels for this kit and I have to say the most informative has been from a user by the name of Siren2173. His videos get into the technical side of the kit and module and he provides several upgrade paths if you still think this kit is not enough for you. So far for me, with the v1.4 udpate, even the hi-hat works well. There are a few quirks that I might could adjust out in a settings menu, so so far so good. My trickiest adjustments have only had to be with my playing technique such as learning exactly how best to trigger the edge and bell on my ride. Now that I know where (and at what angle) to hit, all is good.

I should add that I have made several fine adjustments to the sensitivity and trigger curves for EACH cymbal within the module. That includes each TRIGGER within each cymbal as well. Please note that you may not get the best results straight out of the box. You should be willing to dig into the module to adjust the curve, threshold, and sensitivity of EACH cymbal trigger to really dial this kit into your style. I'm going to repeat that just to stress to you how important this is:

***Please note that you may not get the best results straight out of the box. You should be willing to dig into the module to adjust the curve, threshold, and sensitivity of EACH cymbal trigger to really dial this kit into your style.***

There are a lot of options available to you! You just need to be a bit geeky and curious to plunder the treasures Alesis has given you. I've started building my own custom kit and I love that I can adjust the pitch of each sound. I found the perfect snare but the tuning was too low for me. What's that? I can adjust the tone by +/- 12 steps?! AND fine tune each step even further?! Hell yeah. The module is a beast. Dig around and I swear you will like what you find.

As to the kit itself. The thing is solid. The rack is perfect and the optional bars (mentioned earlier) have helped to customize even more. Heads were a bit awkward for a moment, but hearing the sounds come through with each strike I really do forget that I'm playing mesh. I have experienced NONE of the issues other users are posting online. Some rim sounds are subtle, so please do not assume your triggers are misfiring. Dig into the module, change out a sound to test. Confirm and move on.

The module is pretty nice as well. I do worry about the volume knobs as they wobble under light pressure, but as long as I'm aware I cannot imagine that being a real threat. My kick pedal mounted pretty close to the kick drum rim. At first it was rubbing, so be mindful of how close you clamp down as your chain may be rubbing. I was able to back off and still have enough room for a solid grip. Also regarding the kick, the instructions note to use plastic or wood beater heads as the felt head may wear the head down quickly. My kick has opposing felt/plastic head faces so I just turned them around. Be sure to adjust your kick pedal else you may soon rip a hole through your kick.

Some people have complained about cymbals rotating. As instructed, keep the Alesis logo on the opposite (back) side of the cymbal from yourself. If you look underneath the cymbal, you will see two sort of posts right next to where you mount the cymbal. If you look at the cymbal arm mount, there is a fin where the cymbal would lay. If you position everything correctly, that fin of your mount will slip right between the two posts on the underside of your cymbal. You may have to manually adjust the mount head to get the fin facing back and away from you, but once set up, this will prevent the cymbal from rotating when you play. If it continues, then just tighten the wingnut a bit more. These are not acoustic and as such dot not gain ring by looser mounts.

I love this kit. Please do not let forum warriors convince you that this kit is anything less than great. Since getting this thing setup, I've not spent less than 1-2 hours a day behind it. And as stated earlier, I'm NOT a drummer. That should say a lot to how fun this kit is.

Finally, Alesis Support has been great. They have a community support forum available for public posts. I got a moderator reply in about 3 days. Not too bad. I had also contacted my sales rep regarding the screen dim issue noted earlier. They in turn reached out to Alesis tech support. Within a few days I received a call that I missed due to being at work. The tech followed-up with an email with his contact information stating that he had tried to call, left a voice mail, and requested that I respond with a time that was reasonable for me to speak. Within half an hour of my emailed reply he called me back and we walked through a few questions of mine. He actually put me on hold so that he could walk over to a demo kit to test my experience and to verify my question.

If you care, the v1.2 OS had a different visual representation of EACH trigger you hit so for example if I hit the snare head, the center snare on the display illuminated. If I hit the rim, the outer ring illuminated. That was nice to confirm which trigger fired with each hit. While the updated v1.4 gives a better kit graphic, it also simplified the hit responses to just one illumination of the entire snare, regardless if I'm hitting the head or rim triggers. I was worried this was a glitch. The tech confirmed that the nearby kit was the same. I thought that was damn helpful. Additionally, I've since had two follow-up emails checking in on my support issues. Considering the level of support I've experienced, I truly have no idea where these complaints are coming from.

Bonus round: Due to the non-proprietary input types, you can use mono cable splitters on your TRS inputs. So for example say you want to add another crash. Then simply split your single tom line, routing one mono path to the tom head and the other (what would normally be your tom rim) to an additional cymbal. Granted you only gain one cymbal trigger, you can use it for specialty effects that you wouldn't normally need a bell or edge trigger for any way. You will lose your tom rim trigger but honestly, how often do you need that?! ;)

Good luck with your purchase. I can't recommend it enough.
top_rant.title: 4.5 - Excellent eKit!

top_rant.text:
TLDR:
1. If you have poppy clicky sound that may be due to latency, a USB add on card may be the solution. This was determined after I ran latency tests that showed the USB driver had a 5ms latency, and was resolved with this:
https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B071P5C6CS/ref=ppx_yo_dt_b_asin_title_o06_s00?ie=UTF8&psc=1

2. If you want to use this to break out windows apps to use different inputs and outputs, you are out of luck for now, windows will only default to the first pair of inputs and outputs (It doesn't break out multiple pairs to multiple windows audio devices, like other interfaces such as those made by m-audio or berringer do)

3. After a few months of playing around with this....focusrite support suggested software solutions to compensate for their lack of basic functionality that other similar devices offer, but were of no help actually implementing their suggestion, which in my hours of playing around I found to be inadequate. I cannot tell you how many times this box has blue screened my computer. I've found that even the simplest of use, using the 4 line inputs on the back of the box, has been inadequate as well, as the level on these inputs is way too low for anything I hook up, and there are NO LEVEL ADJUSTMENTS AT ALL FOR THEM!!!! That's pretty basic stuff, setting levels....yet focusrite seemed to have missed out on the memo that inputs need level adjustments! With a complete lack of basic features, a very unstable software, and a device that falls short in every purpose I bought it for, and a software that crashes my machine time and time again, this has to go back, and I cannot in good conscience recommend this device in any way. I am baffled this gets any good reviews at all, I am a professional and have worked with many devices, and this is by far the worst piece of audio equipment I have ever used!

Detailed story below:

If you are reading this, be sure to check back here, as I will be updating this review as my experience unfolds.
I have used many soundcards over the years as part of my hobby of internet streaming. My needs may be unique. But, to boil it down, I need to have 6 inputs and 6 outputs, 3 stereo pairs, so that I can have a setup between my computer and mixing board, so that I have a set of inputs and ouputs for my computer sounds and stereo mix, a set of inputs and outputs for skype to take calls and send a submix that doesn't include skype, and a set of inputs and outputs for teamtalk for co-hosts and to have a submix that doesn't include teamtalk.

My setup up until this point used an m-audio delta 1010lt card. This card is automatically setup as multiple stereo pairs in the sound devices. This is extremely convenient, as it allows for selecting a pair of inputs or outputs from various windows apps rather easily. Simple, basic.....things that are desirable when designing hardware and software.

This focusrite box, shows up as a single device in the sound devices. Out of the box, I basically have a device with 20 inputs and 18 outputs, of which I can only use 2 at a time, which is the most ridiculous thing I've ever heard of. There may be a way to configure this to setup....but now I have to chase down that solution and do complex programming, whereas past products would just do it right out of the box.

But, that's only my secondary problem. Before I can ever bother dealing with the hours of banging my head against that brick wall, there's the first problem of, ALL SOUND THAT COMES FROM MY COMPUTER IS COMPLETELY POPPY AND CLICKY!

For the record, I am running the latest version of Windows 10 64 bit OS. Everything is up to date and current, windows updates are current, drivers are all current, BIOS is current, power options are all set to recommended settings based on focusrite's recommendations (I spent hours reading up and trying to trace down this issue....which, even if I did manage to get it to work, a device that out of the box requires hours of troubleshooting just to get basic functions working doesn't bode well). My machine was built to be an absolute powerhouse. 8 core AMD processor, 32GB of RAM. I have a spare SSD that I put a clean Windows 10 install on, with all updates, and did another clean install of the focusrite software, and found the problems persist. It was as simple as nothing connected to the box except a pair of headphones, and still, any sound from windows came out poppy and clicky.

If my machine isn't enough power for this box, then few machines are.

At this point, I have to say, this device will never be a 5 star device. If, and that's a big if, focusrite support (when they eventually get around to responding to me.....) manage to resolve this issue and get this device working, that out of the box a brand new device, that costs as much as this device does, won't do the most basic of functions and creates hours of nightmares and headaches, nothing will undo that nightmare.

If focusrite does indeed have a winner with this product once something is setup properly (big IF there), then focusrite needs to do some serious research and soul searching into figuring out how to build a device that can consistently and simply just WORK when plugged in! Without that, you're just setting yourself up to look like a bunch of incompetent fools that make crap!

Updates will be posted here after consulting with focusrite support, if they ever get back to me......

....Their support didn't call me after 24 hours. I called them and waited on a short hold. Getting them on the phone isn't hard, but not responding to a ticket for 24 hours is hardly professional.

Before calling support, I did a little searching and troubleshooting, and found a latency test to run, and it was reporting issues suggesting there was throttling on my PC. I never did find anything that was setting any throttling, I believe it was a false diagnosis, but I suppose it's fair that a software will make an educated guess based on the data it has.

The tech I spoke with first suggested that my being on the latest version of Windows 10 is my problem, that they've been having problems with people who upgraded to 1903. He tried to get me on an older driver, but they didn't have an older driver available that was compatible with the 3rd gen 18i20. At some point I mentioned the latency test results, but he was focused on the Windows 10, and basically suggested he was going to forward the information to the development team, and that I'd just have to wait for them to get an updated driver that resolves the issue.

I hung up, figuring I would try to roll back the Windows 10 update, or revert to a previous backup, or find an older installer, to prove or disprove the theory. But the more I thought about it, the more it wouldn't make sense for everyone on the latest Windows 10 update, which has been out for months, to be having noise. So I called back to push for more troubleshooting.

Upon calling back, the tech claimed it's only certain hardware that has the issue with Windows 10 1903. I mentioned I built this machine myself, and would be happy to go over any piece of hardware in my machine with him. He tried to imply that was the problem, saying something to the effect of, we make our devices to work on normal computer. I called that out as pure bs, and we moved on.

I mentioned the latency test, he had me send him results of the tests, and he noticed the usbport.sys driver had a rather high latency, and suggested updating drivers from the manufacturer.

Unfortunately, I bought my motherboard in 2015, and they must have end of life it the next day, as the most up to date drivers for usb and chipsets for my motherboard were from 2014, and for up to Windows 8.1. I had whatever drivers automatically are applied with a Windows 10 installation, it seems they are microsoft drivers, probably something generic. It's worked fine with all my other usb devices (even the dreaded elgato capture device). At this point, the call center was closing, and I'm not that guy that will keep someone past their shift, so we said our goodnights and hung up, so we could each work with what info we captured and resume the next day.

As I thought about it, I thought to myself.....self.....if it's usb drivers that are the problem, and I can't get better drivers for my motherboard....what about that usb 3.0 add on card you recently bought for another machine? So, I borrowed it, threw it in my tower, plugged the focusrite box into that, and voila.....beautiful pop free sound.

https://www.amazon.com/gp/product/B071P5C6CS/ref=ppx_yo_dt_b_asin_title_o06_s00?ie=UTF8&psc=1

So, that did the trick for the latency problems.

However, we were unable to resolve the issue of my needing to route multiple windows apps to different inputs and outputs. Focusrite really has a very narrow view of what an audio interface can be. If you are buying this strictly for multitrack recording in such software that will use the ASIO drivers, great, you're set. If you want to use it for streaming or for purposes to use multiple inputs and outputs with various apps, like skype, teamtalk, to route submixes in and out to each, you are out of luck. Focusrite literally built a box, with 18 inputs and 20 outputs, that will only let you use 2 of each (1 stereo pair of each) as windows sound devices. Which means, for my purposes, it's worthless.

Such a disappointing end. I'm back where I started, on an m-audio delta 1010lt with way outdated drivers that is a ticking time bomb, with no suitable replacement in sight.

Focusrite shows promise. Great hardware, a proven track record of support as far as continuing to develop software for older devices.....but severely lacking in software capabilities.
top_rant.title: Awful setup experience, very demanding hardware, had to find my own fix, limited capabilities

top_rant.text:
Update 20220309
Below was my 3 star update. I just bumped it to 5. It's been stable. I guess the last time I had a problem was when I put that review update, 2 years ago. ... though after reading below, I think I stopped doing wifi, and I only use the laptop that it's connected to, for browser console. Nevertheless, it's been stable.

Update 20200410: I was working through a Kenny Gioia Reaper Mania video, setting up 10 inputs on one drum track with output routing to separate Reaper faders...then my 828es just hung...it...just...hung. Had to power cycle it.
MOTU Support update 20200213: I'm not sure if it's the nasty-gram I left here on amazon or the nasty-gram I left on my MOTU support ticket, but I did get a call back, and the guy was super nice and helpful--to his credit. He seems very sincere, and we talked through my unit's issue of dropping sound. He gave me some troubleshooting steps which I've tried. My issue is that when I connect a device, via isolated Wifi--828es has ethernet cable to AirPort Extreme, then I connect Galaxy S8 or Macbook Pro to the web browser console/mixer via Wifi. Either device will seemingly crash the audio output. HOWEVER, one of the troubleshooting steps, when main output sound stops, was to route an input mic direct to hardware outs--in my case, to a couple aux sends going to a headphone amp. That indeed did work, so I'll continue working with support to see what the root cause is. You can find my work-around below, which is to make my Windows 10 Pro laptop as the web server, that passes through to the 828es, which seems stable...another problem of mine is that I have a regular job, so getting support on the line while troubleshooting in real-time is also not helping...but that's not MOTU's fault. My gripe was that I was responding to an open ticket that went ignored for what I considered to be too long. But I don't know how understaffed, overworked, underpaid their support might be--I only say that because that seems to be the way of things, everywhere...
Another update, a day later 2020-02-09: Summary, I really love this MOTU 828es, when it works, and I may have just figured out a work-around. I actually think it's a 5 Star piece of gear, but I'm so disappointed in their lack of support that I'm leaving this as a 1 star to see if it gets their attention, because I opened a support ticket online that went unanswered for something like a week before I called support, waited for half an hour, and did get a nice guy who went through the script...and he replied a bit shortly after, but then went radio silent again, regarding my online ticket updates...I guess you HAVE to call them to get their attention... So, new news here, and work-around: Since having the MOTU 828es on its own Wifi (an Apple Airport Extreme) would crash when my Samsung Galaxy S8 would connect, with either Firefox or Chrome browser--to be clear, the 828es was on it’s an isolated Wifi network, because the only physical network cable in the Airport Extreme was the MOTU 828es, not internet, no router; so I would change the wifi network on my smartphone, then access the 828es on the browser, and it kinda worked, until it didn't randomly. Not sure if having the 828es on its own wifi network was a problem, but regardless, the main outs would stop sending signal. i.e. a microphone would show signal (mic -> profire 2626 -> ADAT out -> 828es ADAT in), the signal showed level as an OPT (optical) input on LCD and the MOTU touch console would show input signal on the Thunderbolt connected PC, but as soon as smartphone connected via web browser, main out sound would stop--not every single time, but more often than is anywhere near acceptable. And, this happened on both USB 2.0 and Thunderbolt connection.

SOLUTION!!! (seems like this is a solution--but a work-around)

I have a new-ish Windows 10 2-in-1 Asus laptop, with a Thunderbolt connection. I’m not sure if this would work with Windows 10 Home--maybe it would, because the Windows firewall looked like it would allow ingress connections. But I just upgraded to Windows 10 Pro, because I found an article that said if you have a legit license key of Win 7 Pro/Ultimate, then you could upgrade Windows 10 from Home to Pro for free--and that did indeed work. I allowed incoming connections but things still were not working--nor was my new feature of Windows Remote Desktop working either...so the last hurdle was to find the Windows Network Profile--left click my Wifi icon, and on the wifi connection that I was on, there’s a “Properties” link, and the Network Profile is there; I think I originally clicked it as “Public” to block everything, as a paranoid IT guy. Odd though, I allowed RDP and “MOTU Discovery“ “MOTUProAudioHTTPServer“ “MOTUProAudioPort“ through the firewall for both Private/Public, but I digress … So, I changed the Network Profile of my home Wifi to “Private” and that did allow my Samsung Galaxy S8 to connect to the wifi IP of my laptop on tcp port 1280, so in a browser, it would be something like this (your subnet may be different) http://192.168.1.100:1280 --so the Windows 10 Pro laptop is now a web server, that passes the connection through to the 828es...however, in the firewall where you “Allow an app or feature through Windows Defender Firewall” I noticed that it kept adding additional/redundant MOTU items, specifically “MOTU Discovery“ “MOTUProAudioHTTPServer“ “MOTUProAudioPort“. Being a little tweaky about extra redundant crap, I decided to “Remove” all the extras, then I broke connectivity...a reboot did not fix. After checking the Windows firewall again, I noticed that the checkbox to the left of the MOTU items was not checked for some. I checked those, unchecked the Public boxes, and when I went back one last time, there was still one extra MOTU Discovery, but I removed that and I think I’m in business. So, in the end, I’m using my Windows 10 laptop, connected to 828es via Thunderbolt (yes, Thunderbolt on Windows [Asus]), with the Windows 10 laptop as the http server, and the Windows 10 firewall is passing the MOTU ProAudioPort (tcp/1280) through the laptop to the MOTU 828es, so I can connect on my Android Galaxy S8 smartphone via the web browser Touch Console, using the laptop IP (your subnet may be different) http://192.168.1.100:1280 ...and this seems to be stable, perhaps because all connections funnel through the laptop?

What follows is my latest update on my open support ticket 2020-02-08: So, here we are again...my last update to this ticket was almost 3 weeks ago, without a response. And it just happened AGAIN! Can I speak with the next tier of support? I definitely have a software problem with my 828es, it's a pattern and I think my troubleshooting would be helpful to your developers. For weeks now, I've just been sitting at the laptop, NOT using my Samsung Galaxy S8 for the Touch Console. Today, I was across the room, playing drums along with a tune. I had the monitor speakers on, but I wanted to turn them off, and use headphones. I plugged in my headphones and heard the tune loud & clear, so I connected my Galaxy S8 to the isolated WiFi that the 828es is on, then as soon as the Touch Console appeared, I hit the Device virtual button, so I could turn off the 'A' Monitors remotely, so I didn't have to get up & walk across the room to do so...that was one of the appealing features of buying this gear...but AS SOON AS I HIT THE DEVICE BUTTON, SOUND FROM THE COMPUTER STOPPED. and AGAIN, no ASIO adjustments would bring the audio back, so I was forced to reboot the 828es--HOWEVER, IT STILL DID NOT PRODUCE AUDIO AFTER REBOOT, BECAUSE MY GALAXY S8 CONNECTED TO IT AGAIN, SO I GOT THE GALAXY S8 OFF THE WIFI THAT THE 828es IS CONNECTED TO, REBOOTED THE 828es AGAIN, THEN THE AUDIO WORKED, THE MICROPHONE SENT SIGNAL AND THE OUTPUTS OF THE LCD SCREEN STARTED SHOWING OUTPUT ; so it's not just computer audio that is not flowing, a microphone that I can see signal on, in the Touch Console is not coming through, so it's not the computer, I don't think it's ASIO drivers, because the microphone should work as a stand-alone mixer/PA system--again, there's mic signal in the console, but no audio output. Furthermore, the Touch Console shows signal on the "Main" stereo output, but the 828es LCD screen does not show any output signal from the mic. I hit the 'A' monitor virtual button out of curiosity, and it did disable the 'A' monitors, as evidenced by the 'A' physical button back light going dark. And I'm still able to enable/disable either A/B monitors, but NO SOUND is coming through. So, respectfully, what do I have to do to get a response here, but more importantly, what is MOTU doing about software updates. I have to believe that I'm not the only person having this issue...maybe loads of people are, and that's why you're swamped and can't keep up with tickets? ...and as I was sitting here, I figured I'd try yet again, and indeed, AS SOON AS MY GALAXY S8 CONNECTS TO THE TOUCH CONSOLE--AS IN, I DIDN'T TOUCH ANY THING IN THE TOUCH CONSOLE--THE AUDIO STOPS WORKING. AGAIN...AUDIO IS FLOWING FROM MY MICROPHONE, BUT AS SOON AS I TRY TO OPEN THE TOUCH CONSOLE WEB BROWSER ON MY GALAXY S8, AUDIO STOPS WORKING. PLEASE ESCALATE THIS.
Original review here: I do love this piece of gear. I'm just a hobbyist, but I wanted to upgrade my home studio. For many years I had a bit of a hodgepodge with an old Midi Man Delta 1010 and a Profire 2626, for a 16 (or 18 with SPDIF) track studio, on Cakewalk Sonar 8.5--now switching to Reaper. I've had old band mates over for jams but ran out of inputs, since I have a fully mic'd drum kit. I wanted 24+ tracks, with MIDI, so this seemed to be the best piece of gear, and it really is. 8 1/4" TRS ins, plus 2 combo XLR/TRS on the front gives 10 analog inputs, plus stereo SPDIF (12 total inputs). Then I bought a 2nd Profire 2626 because they go for around $150 used on Ebay, which gives 16 tracks of ADAT optical into the 828es, so now I have a 28 track studio. The software mixer on the 828es is so freaking cool, such an upgrade from the bare bones Delta 1010 or Profire 2626 mixers that had nothing but volume. The 828es mixer has gate, parametric eq, compression--all per channel--and a reverb effect that's pretty good. Then they add on a software model of the Teletronix™ LA-2A optical leveling amplifier. Routing is super cool. Control is web interface. This works for me on an Asus 2-in-1 Windows laptop that actually has a Thunderbolt interface--yeah, Thunderbolt on a Windows box, and it works with the 828es! Oh, and I almost forgot to mention the 14 aux sends, which I combine to make 7 stereo aux (headphone) mixes. So crazy cool. So, I love these toys! So now the gripe. It has crashed on me a handful of times, and when I opened a support ticket on MOTU's site, they didn't get back to me after waiting over a week, so I called, and waited on hold for about a half hour before I got a guy. He found my ticket and gave me pointers. I started on the USB 2.0 interface, then bought the wrong "Thunderbolt" cable (you actually need an Apple brand Thunderbolt 3 (USB C) to Thunderbolt 2 (female) adapter, then the older Thunderbolt 2 cable...but it works), however I still get this thing to crash on me...I think it crashes when I connect my Android phone to the 828es web browser mixer, when I'm across the room. That was a feature that I really wanted, the ability to control the mixer on any device...and it mostly works, and I'm not sure if the screen orientation bugs out (when I turn the phone between landscape/portrait). But it seems stable if I only use my laptop. If I were some pro studio, I'm not so sure about the 828es stability...I don't ever recall the Delta 1010 or Profire 2626 locking up & crashing...I'm hoping that they'll come out with a software/firmware update that will fix that. Again, it's been good & stable on the laptop, so I do really dig this box. Then you can go crazy buy/connect other MOTU devices via computer cables for a bigger rig--but that's a whole other thing to dig into. Check out MOTU videos of these devices and software mixer on youtube.

top_screaming.text:
I HAVE BEEN A DJ FOR MANY YEARS AND APPROX. 8 YEAR AGO I ORDERED A SET OF NADY DKW VHS WIRELESS MICS, B/D FREQ. I SOON ORDERED ANOTHER SET (P/R FREQ.) AS A SECOND STANDBY SET. NOT KNOWING HOW LONG THE PRODUCT WOULD LAST I THEN ORDERED ANOTHER SET OF B/D FREQ. TO BE USED AS A STANDBY SPARE SET. I HAVE HAD GREAT LUCK WITH THIS PRODUCT AND I DO ALL KINDS OF SHOWS UNDER SOMETIMES VERY DIFFICULT CONDITIONS. I HAVE NEVER USED THE SPARE SET YET SINCE THE FIRST SET IS STILL WORKING PERFECTLY! THE SOUND IS GREAT, AND YES I DO SHOWS IN LARGE THEATERS, OUTDOORS, LARGE HALLS AND A MULITUDE OF OTHER PLACES.THEY WORKED SO WELL WHEN OTHER DJ'S HEARD THEM, I ORDERED SETS FOR THEM AND FOR LOCAL CHURCHES! I HAVE READ THE REVIEWS ABOUT THIS PRODUCT AND CAN SEE THAT MOST TIMES MOST OF THE PEOPLE DO NOT KNOW HOW TO USE OR CARE FOR THIS TYPE EQUIPMENT! THIS PRODUCT DOES HAVE IT'S DRAWBACKS AND I WILL MENTION THEM LATER. OVERALL LET'S FACE IT! YOU ARE ONLY BUYING A INEXPENSIVE SET OF WIRELESS MICS!
THE DRAWBACKS AS I HAVE FOUND ARE FIRST, THE CASING CAP AROUND THE BATTERY IS VERY THIN, I HAVE TAPED AROUND MY UNITS WHERE THEY TEND TO CRACK. SECOND,IF DROPPED OR BUMPED HARD, THE ACTUAL MIC COMPONENT UNDER THE WINDSHIELD WILL MOVE FORWARD AND DOES NOT ALLOW THE ON/OFF SWITCH TO FUNCTION PROPERLY (YOU CAN UNSCREW THE WIND SHIELD AND PUSH THE MIC UNIT BACK INTO PLACE)I CURRED THIS PROBLEM BY USING A SODER PENCIL AND MOULDIND THE MIC UNIT TO THE SURROUNDING CASING AND THEN REPLACING THE WIND SHIELD CAP. DROPPING ANY TYPE MIC WILL CAUSE DAMAGE USUALLY!!
NEXT I WOULD LIKE TO SEE THE MANUFACTOR PLACE A DURABLE, DIFFERENT COLORED TAPE STRIP, (PREFERED REFLECTIVE TYPE)AROUND EACH DIFFERENT FREQ. MIC, TO ALLOW THE DJ WHO MAY BE CONTROLLING THE MIXER TO BE ABLE TO SEE WHICH PERSON HAS WHICH MIC IN ORDER FOR HIM/HER TO ADJUST THE INPUT ACCORDINGLY!(AROUND THE BATTERY CASING WOULD BE GREAT!)
AS MENTIONED BEFORE, I PURCHASED A B/D FREQ SET AND A P/R SET. THESE ARE SUPPOSE TO BE DIFFERENT FREQ. HOWEVER ONE MIC ON THE P/R SET WILL LIGHT UP THE INDICATOR LIGHT ON THE B/D SET AND ACTUALLY OPERATE AND CAUSE INTERFERENCE! I BELIEVE THIS IS BECAUSE THE TWO BASE UNIT MAY BE TOO CLOSE TOGETHER. I HAVE NEVER REALLY TRIED TO EXPERIMENT VERY MUCH WITH THIS SINCE I HAVE ALL MY DJ GEAR MOUNTED ON MY MOBILE DJ EQUIPMENT STAND.
I NOTED EARILER THAT SEVERAL REMARKS ABOUT HAVING TO PLACE THE MIC TOUCHING THE MOUTH TO BE HEARD. THIS IS MOST LIKELY DUE TO A BATTERY THAT HAS GOTTEN LOW ON POWER AND ALSO I FIND THE MIC'S WORK BEST APPROX. 1 TO 1 1/2 INCH FROM YOUR MOUTH AND SPOKEN INTO DIRECTLY STRAIGHT LINE MOUTH TO MIC NOT TILTED THIS WILL PREVENT MOST FEEDBACK FROM ANY OTHER SOURCE.I HAVE FOUND THAT IF YOU MAX OUT THE BASE CONTROLS THIS WILL ALSO CAUSE A DISTORTED SOUND, IT IS BEST TO PLACE THE BASE VOLUME CONTROLS APPROX 3/4 WAY AROUND. TYPICALLY DIFFERENT PEOPLE WILL SING AT DIFFERENT LEVELS OF VOLUME. IT IS BEST TO ASK THE PERSON TO SPEAK OR SING LOUDLY DIRECTLY INTO THE MIC THEREFORE THE DJ CAN ADJUST THE LEVEL ACCORDINGLY AS NEEDED.
REMEMBER, THIS IS A VERY SENITIVE PIECE OF EQUIPMENT AND MUST BE CARED FOR PROPERLY. MOST OF THE COMPLAINTS THAT I HAVE READ, WERE CUSTOMER RELATED AND PRODUCT NOT BEING USED CORRECTLY! THIS PRODUCT WILL GIVE YOU YEARS OF DEPENDABLE SERVICE IF USED CORRECTLY. ONE MORE NOTE, PLACE YOUR UNIT AWAY FROM ANY SPEAKER, FAN UNITS, A/C UNITS, MICROWAVES OR ANY PRODUCT THAT MAY CREATE A NOISE/FREQ. INTERFERENCE, FOR BEST RESULTS.
I WOULD ORDER MORE SETS OF THESE CORDLESS MICS (IT IS GREAT NOT HAVING TO SPLICE CABLES) HOWEVER I WILL HAVE TO WAIT UNTIL THE ONES I HAVE QUIT! I WOULD RECCOMEND THEM TO ANYONE WHO WANTS A GREAT SET AT A VERY REASONABLE PRICE.

I BOUGHT THIS FOR MY KARAOKE BUSINESS. I'VE ONLY DONE 4 GIGS WITH IT SO FAR. THE SOUNS QUALITY WAS SUPRISINGLY GREAT AND BETTER THAN MY PREVIOUS MICS (SURE SM 58) BUT YOU HAVE TO PUT THE MIC A SLIGHTLY CLOSER TO YOUR FACE. THERE'S A LITTLE MORE OF A POPPING SOUND ON WORDS THAT HAVE THE LETTER "P" IN IT BUT HALFWAY DECENT SINGERS START TO SOFTEN THE LETTER AFTER NOTICING THE DIFFERENCE. THE RANGE IS PERFECT EVEN WHILE WALKING OR RUNNING AND EVEN WITHOUT CONNECTING THE LITTLE ANTENAE IN THE BACK (THE FUTHEST I'VE TESTED WAS A LITTLE UNDER 100 FT THROUGH SEVERAL WALLS), BUT I STILL PUT IT ON. THE STANDBY MODE IS ANOYING AND NOT NEEDED. THE RED LIGHT SHOULD BE GREEN BECAUSE CUSTOMER THINK RED MEANS "STANDBY", "OFF" OR "DYING BATTERY".

THE BEST THING ABOUT THIS PRODUCT IS THAT THERE IS NO HUMMING OR BACKGROUND NOISE WHEN NOBODY'S SINGING AND THERE IS ALMOST NO FEEDBACK AT ALL. I NO LONGER WORRY THAT THE SINGER IS GOING TO WANDER TOO CLOSE TO THE SPEAKER. I WAS ABOUT TO BUY A $100 FEEDBACK ELIMINATOR BEFORE I GOT THESE MICS. MY SET DID NOT PICKUP ANY RADIO STATIONS OR OTHER BROADCAST OF ANY KIND. THE BASE UNIT IS RACK-MOUNTABLE AND LIGHT WEIGHT. WORKS FINE WOTHOUT THE ANTENAES EXTENDED. THERE ARE FOUR LEDS SHOWING YOU WHICH MIC IS WHICH. THE BOX THE SYSTEM COMES IN IS SMALL AND HAS A HANDLE - I USE IT TO CARRY THE MICS AFTER I STUFFED A PIECE OF BUBBLE WRAP IN THE SPACE WHERE THE BASE UNIT USED TO BE.

JUST LIKE THE OTHER REVIEWERS I PUT BLACK TAPE OVER THE BATTERY DOOR (THAT ALWAYS GETS LOST NO MATTER WHAT THE DEVICE IS) AND THREE OF THE MICS WORK PERFECT. ONE OF THEM LOST VOLUME (I KEEP IT TO MC & LET THE CUSTOMER USE THE OTHERS) BUT I CAN ADJUST THE RF (IT JUST A SCREW INSIDE THE MIC YOU TURN LEFT OR RIGHT) OR THE VOLUME ON THE OTHERS TO EVEN THEM OUT (THE MICS CAN ALL BE ON ONE CHANNEL OR FOUR SEPERATE CHANNELS) LATER. THEY ARE MADE IN CHINA SO THE PLASTIC IS A LITTLE FLIMSY BUT NOT MUCH AND THEY HAVE A COOL SOFT FINISH. I DON'T HEAR THE BATTERIES RATTLING AROUND. BATTERY LIFE IS ABOUT 16 HRS. I WOULD RECOMEND GETTING A RECHARGEABLE BATTERY SYSTEM - THE BATTERIES THE MICS CAME WITH LEAKED IN ONE MIC (THINK THIS MIGHT BE WHAT LESSEND THE VOLUME ON ONE MIC). EVEN IF ONLY 3 WORK (I DIDN'T EXPECT ALL 4 TO WORK BASED ON PREVIOUS CUSTOMER REVIEWS) - THE SYSTEM IS STILL A GREAT BUY FOR ME CONSIDERING I PAID $100 FOR EACH OF MY PREVIOUS MICS. I'M EVEN GONNA BUY A BACKUP SET!

top_screaming.text:
I HAVE BEEN RECORDING WITH TASCAM MULTITRACK RECORDERS BEGINNING WITH A TASCAM 34 4 TRACK 10" REEL-TO-REEL IN 1984....THEN I MOVED UP TO THE TASCAM 38 8 TRACK WITH 1/2" 10" REELS. AND 2 TASCAM DX-4D NOISE REDUCTION UNITS.
I MADE EXCELLENT RECORDINGS ON THOSE MACHINES AND THEY WERE VERY DURABLE...
IN THE LATE '90'S I BOUGHT 2 TASCAM DA-38'S DIGITAL AUDIO TAPE RECORDERS AND HAD THEM SYNCHRONIZED TOGETHER FOR 16 TRACK AND HAD THEM IN A FLIGHT CASE FOR RECORDING LIVE GIGS....AND ALL THOSE UNITS USED REMOVABLE MEDIA... AND THAT WAS EASY TO RECORD DIFFERENT BANDS AND I JUST HAD THEM GET THEIR OWN ROLL OF TAPE OR DAT TAPES FOR THEIR MASTERS...

BUT IN THE '2000'S DIGITAL RECORDERS CAME OUT AND I GOT A ROLAND VS2480CD FOR THE MAIN RECORDING UNIT, AND A KORG D-3200 DIGITAL RECORDER FOR PORTABILITY WITH LIVE RECORDINGS....
HOWEVER, AFTER MANY RECORDING SESSIONS, THEIR WAS NO WAY TO GIVE THE BAND THEIR MASTER RECORDING, AS IT WAS ON MY HARD DRIVES... I HAD TO BURN THEM A CD.
NOT WANTING TO MESS WITH THEIR ORIGINAL SONG TRACKS TRYING TO TRANSFER THEM TO SOME OTHER FORMAT,,,, AND TAKING HOURS TO BURN THEM CD'S...THE HARD DRIVES QUICKLY FILLED UP,,, AND THE ONLY SOLUTION IS TO TRANSFER THE RECORDINGS TO SOME OTHER FORMAT AND THEN DELETE THEM OFF THE HARD DRIVE.....OR YOU CAN'T USE THE RECORDERS WITH FULL HARD DRIVES...

NOW THAT TASCAM HAS A QUALITY RECORDER WITH REMOVABLE SD CARDS, THIS AGAIN, MAKES IT POSSIBLE, LIKE TAPE REELS, TO RECORD A BAND AND JUST GIVE THEM AN SD CARD AS THEIR MASTER AND YOU DON'T FILL UP ANY HARD DRIVES THAT STOP YOUR MACHINE DEAD IN IT'S TRACKS.

I HIGHLY RECOMMEND THE TASCAM UNITS, THEY ARE SOLID, AND THE NEW SD CARD STORAGE MAKES THIS A UNIT I CAN USE FOR YEARS WITHOUT FILLING UP HARD DRIVES...JUST POP IN A NEW SD CARD FOR EACH PROJECT, AND POP IN A DIFFERENT ONE FOR YOUR OTHER RECORDING SESSIONS...GREAT!
top_screaming.title: Nice to Have REMOVABLE SD CARD MEDIA

THIS IS MY FIRST VIDEOKE, SO I DIDN'T REALLY KNOW WHAT TO GET THE ONES MY SISTERS AND BROTHERS HAVE WERE DIFFERENT KIND AND THEY'RE BIG. I SEARCHED IN THE INTERNET AND READ REVIEWS. I CHOSE THIS BECAUSE I WANT TAGALOG SONGS IN MY  VIDEOKE AS WELL AS IT WAS CHEAPER THAN THE OTHER ONES- (I JUST WANTED A VIDEOKE I CAN LEARN TO SING-A-LONG- I DIDN'T WANT TO PAY TOO MUCH AT THAT TIME.)  I ALSO LIKE THE SONGS AVAILABLE IN THIS VIDEOKE.  WELL I WAS VERY HAPPY WHEN I RECEIVED MY VIDEOKE, IT WAS SMALL, SO YOU VAN CARRY IT AROUND AND IT DOESN'T NEED MUCH SPACE. top_exclaim.text: I'm a beginner, but, I have been playing flute for about 20 years, and read music. I recently took up the violin, since there are more seats in orchestras for violins than flutes. I made the mistake of buying a student violin for $300, spent $40 for fine tuners, and it still sounded tinny. I went to an antique violin store and traded in my new violin (only got $150 credit for it) for an antique $800 violin. I then did my homework and was about to buy Dominant strings with a different gold E string, and then I found a video with a guy telling everyone that he switched to these (same company as Dominant), and loved them so much he would marry them! LOL! So, I bought these for my violin, had them put on, and WOW!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!! Beautiful! My violin teacher loves them more than his Dominants. He had tried the Infeld blue, didn't care for them, but the RED has the gold E string that sounds so beautiful! I will never play on other strings. When I upgrade to a new violin when I get better, I will use these strings. WORD OF CAUTION:  Do NOT use cheap rosin on your bow with these strings!!!!!!!!!!! If you are using cheap rosin, watch the youtube video on how to remove rosin from your bows hair (which is what I did) and then purchase the Pirastro Goldflex Rosin here on Amazon to use on these strings. Butter! Worth the work, you will sound like a pro. Be sure to use a clean tissue to wipe your strings off after playing so that it doesn't build up on them. BTW, I have had about 12 lessons, and I practiced a lot, and am now playing in a Volunteer Orchestra (the are good!) 2nd violin. Do yourself a favor, buy these! Especially if you have a tinny sounding violin, this will give you a rich, warm and beautiful sound. Well worth the money! I'm buying them for my violin teacher for Christmas. He liked them just as much as I did. Happy playing!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
top_exclaim.title: I'm a beginner, and wow! What a difference!

top_five_star_obscene.text:
If you're a musician, you know how cable shopping is. It's a crap shoot. Your choices are either a.) go down to the local music store and pay way too much or b.) order online and hope what you get isn't absolute crap. Over the years, you end up with a hodgepodge of cables that have lasted over a decade without so much as a cold solder, and cables that were such crap to begin with the only thing keeping you from using them as ties to hold up your tomato plants is that you're too lazy to garden and actually grow tomatoes.

These cables are the good kind. They're not super heavy duty, but thick enough. They're not the ones that seem like cell phone charger cable with XLR jacks (if you've ordered cheap cables more than a handful of times you know what I'm talking about). And the jacks work. No issues with any of them being misaligned, breaking, etc. I'm only a year or so in but so far so good. Also, they're plain jane which I like. No colored shrink wrap, overly fancy XLR connectors, obnoxious branding, or anything of the sort.
top_five_star_obscene.title: After a year they continue to function perfectly.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
I've played the same Fender USA strat since 2011, and I've played it a lot. It's the first thing I would grab if my house was on fire. With that said, I didn't really want anything to replace it, but I wanted something smaller and easier to play in the bedroom and other places that make a smaller form a little more bearable (makes it so much easier to get into those port-o-potties).

Anyway, it IS much easier to play in more cramped spaces, BUT, holy crap is this thing fun to play. And it plays GREAT. The feel of it is awesome. The only downsides I have, which aren't enough to care about for me for this guitar, are the neck width (a little small for my hands, but I have big hands), and maybe the dead, bland feel of the wood in this guitar. It's still worth the money, but there's a way that good wood sounds when a guitar vibrates, and this once definitely has zero of that. But that isn't going to affect playability or how it sounds to a degree. And for the money, it's just fine. In fact that's maybe not even fair to gripe about at all with a guitar this cheap.

ALL new guitars need a proper setup. It also takes a few hours of playing to get it broken in a little bit. I highly suggest buffing out/polishing the frets (this took the most time, but it made the most difference for me), NEW STRINGS, adjust pickups, adjust truss rod. Besides the fret buffing, bridge pickup being too low, and a little neck relief added, mine was actually in great shape.

I also adjusted the bridge saddles a bit to match the radius of the fret board. I am only saying all of the setup stuff because I am seeing a lot of negative comments about this guitar being junk because it plays like crap. It WILL play like crap out of the box. You have to do more than tune it... It's a music instrument. It desperately needs intonation and new strings. Ibanez did this at the factory but that was in China, and it happened many months before it showed up in your hands. It's easy to do, and it will get this guitar up to its potential. I can't believe the build quality of this thing for 150 bucks. I highly recommend it!
top_five_star_obscene.title: This thing is great

top_five_star_obscene.text:
These headphones are old school CooooooL retro style which is what i like about them. VERY, VERY good external sound isolation, not sound cancelation! You can't hear shit with these things on which means you have to lift the no-lead side ear cup to converse with anyone. But thats fine. i don't want to talk much any way while doing music.

Very comfortable, especially if you have large ears or sensitive ears that get sore from direct pressure contact against them, like mine. I HATE things pushing against my ears and bending the shit out of them! Your ear sits completely inside the cups so you can wear them for hours no worries.

I play bass guitar using these headies and they have REALLY good bottom end audio frequency ranges and top ends are very solid also. The input jack has a neat wind on wind off set-up, with the 3.5 mini jack inside the main jack for ipods or tablets, or what ever. The Single sided lead is way better than those left and right Y shaped leads that get in the f.....g way for everything and anything!

Downsides? The only one i could think of was the curly spring jack lead, while VERY stretchy, it's too short for walking ALL around a room playing guitar, but its perfect for MOST uses because it doesn't tangle around your feet trying to trip you over at only 18-24 inches long unstretched. They'd probably work perfectly for an ipod while walking, running or riding a bike but you wont hear shit from outside of these things so don't get run over.

Quality? Yep! Way better construction than my mid level Sennheisers, which were made of 100% cheapo crapy plastic but still sounded good. These are quality WELL beyond the measley price asked for them and represent great value for people who aren't suckers for similar high performance headphones from trendy Corporate branded marketing hype products at 7-10 times the price. Get'em!
top_five_star_obscene.title: headfone review for CAD DH 100

top_five_star_obscene.text: Every guitar is different no matter who makes it, every top has its own grain, and the wood makes the sound in any guitar. All kinds of things affect the sound of a guitar including how many coats of finish laquer are sprayed on it, too many coats will kill the tone.if you play 10 of these you will find that 2 stand out above the other 8 and that 4 will sound like utter crap, and the other 4 will be mediocre at best. This is just the nature of acoustic instruments, they all have their own personality and none are going to be exactly the same. Set up also affects them in many ways. If you set it up with low action , you will nkt be able to play with as much volume and therefore you will not get as much tone out of it. If4 you change the saddle and string nut to bone you will get more tone than with the cheap plastic. If you pick a guitar with a tight grain in the top, you are more likely to get a better sounding guitar th7an one with6 wider grains. If you use phosphor bronze strings like D'Addario's , you will get a crisper more responsive tone. In other words with a little education about what makes a guitar sound good you can pick a good one from any manufacturer who uses real solid spruce tops and some descent qaulity strings. Now, with all that said, I personally recomend playing a few of them and picking the best one out of the bunch. I did that at my local music store and the one i picked sounds as good as some Martin D-18's that i have owned in the past. But of course i have set it up with a slightly high action, some medium bronze D'Addario strings , and a bone saddl and nut, so now it is a little different from what it was straight out of the box, and it sounds great. By the way i have been playing multiple acoustic instruments since i was a teenager, i grew up around proffessional musicians and my father is a Luthier, which means he builds instruments for those of you who dont know the lingo. In short this is a great guitar for the price, it needs a little work straight out of the box, but picking an acoustic instrument is like picking a girlfriend,  pretty is as pretty does and they all have different attitudes any where from pretty and sweet to looks good but sounds like a train wreck. You cant fix crappy wood. All manufacturers use whatever wood is on top of the stack for each guitar, gone a1re th6e days when a small luthier shop would hand pick quality wood4s for each individual guitar and try to turn out only the best. Unfotunately even Gibson , Martin, Fender, and all the other major players build just as many crappy instruments as they good ones. Its all about Dollar bills now, no more pride in their craftsmanship. So go through them and pick the ones that accidentally got some good wood and you will get a better guitar
top_five_star_obscene.title: if you play 10 of these you will find that 2 stand out above the other 8 and that 4 will sound like utter crap

top_five_star_obscene.text: Well, my SG special arrived yesterday. I must admit, when I ordered this guitar I wasn't expecting much from it. I have way more expensive guitars, I basically just wanted a decent electric guitar I could just leave around the house, without having a mild heart attack if my 10 year old should happen to pick it up and start wailing on it. Well, guess what much to my surprize, this thing is amazing! It literally plays and sounds better than my Epi LP custom pro, my Epi Casino Coupe and my both my Strats. I don't understand how Epiphone can even offer a guitar of this quality at such a low price? It boggles the mind. First of all, the description says the body is Laminated Alder, wrong!!! This body is full blown Solid Mahogany! And I have the cherry model, which is wonderfully transparent so you can see every beautiful grain in it. The neck solid maple, again you can see every grain. As for the hardware, better than I expected. Almost every review says the tuning machines are crap, cheap plastic over metal. I even ordered a set of Grovers before I even got it, expecting crap. And guess what, these tuners are the Premium die cast 14:1's. Not at all crap as I expected. So needless to say, the Grovers will sit in a box for a long time before I even think about replacing them. So, all in all I would say to anyone concidering this instrument,  unless you have an aversion to falling in love with a guitar that doesn't cost $3500, or a need to impress your friends with a "name" on the headstock?
