top_five_star_obscene.text: So far nut and bridge pins were drop in replacements on my Alvarez guitar. And they make a positive difference for sure. The original nut was crap, and now this thing sounds better with $5 d'addarios on it than it did before with elixirs! Some of you probably don't like elixirs, but given my tone preferences, that is pretty damn impressive to me. The original nut was just dulling the crap out of the sound of the strings and I knew it needed to be replaced as soon as I got the guitar. And I can't tell you how grateful I am for the fact that these were drop in replacements as I do not have guitar tools or nut files and have never filed a nut before. I didn't have get ripped off by a guitar douche for something I could do myself YAY! One look at this nut compared to the original let me know it was almost a match but far superior. Just so happy with this for the money, the nut and the bridge pins alone are worth it. The saddle will definitely have to be filed unless you like slide a whole lot, it's way too tall, but the length should be right for most cheap import acoustics (the ones that need it the most) at least. The saddle is the right length for my Alvarez and my Fender Sonoran acoustic, and the compensation for intonation on it seems right. In the pictures I provided, the detached nut is the original. You may not be able to see, but the slots are filed poorly, and it is much uglier than the bone nut. Very happy.

top_five_star_obscene.text:
I purchased this cable to be able to get a balanced audio connection between a television and a pair of studio monitors I had that were just sitting around collecting dust. The monitors have unbalanced RCA inputs, balanced TRS inputs, and balanced XLR inputs. The television in question has HDMI inputs (one of which is an ARC connection... however, ARC will only work to output audio to a device with the capability to accept ARC audio, which essentially makes it an ARC device), an optical or toslink output, and a 3.5mm audio output (the 3.5mm connection is more widely known as a headphone connection, however, it can be used for much more than a set of headphones), and that is it. There are no other connections for audio on the back panel (or anywhere else for that matter) of the television. This has become relatively common place among television manufacturers these days, as analog devices have long since begun to take a back seat to their much more prevalent digital counterparts. And it is just kind-of bass-ackwards and largely unnecessary for manufacturers to go out of their way including analog audio connections on their televisions when the vast majority of consumers will never use them, let alone look at them or even know what they are called or for. Anyway, after listening to my wife's television programs sound like crap for so long, because of the fact that the speakers in this television are garbage, and one of them is even blown, I believe, I started trying to come up with a solution. However, I knew I needed a solution that did not A: involve me ripping the aforementioned television off of it's mount and throwing it through the nearest window, or B: involve any type of soundbar. I think it's fairly obvious that the cost-effectiveness of choice A would be in the negative, seeing as how it would likely involve having to call a home improvement company for repairs to the window, at the very least. And choice B, well, a soundbar would be just as ineffective in the long haul, which wouldn't be too long at all. Because soundbars are garbage. Sorry to any soundbar owners reading this, but the truth is, if this is you, then you should count yourself double-lucky. First because it helped to guide you toward making the right choice, and purchasing this cable (so long as you are certain that it has the proper connections necessary for it's intended use), because it is a fantastic cable. And second, because if you are paying attention, then this review will have helped open your eyes to the extremely poor business practices of many huge (and some not so huge) companies who are making millions off of selling junk to consumers, simply by manufacturing it in a form factor which can easily be mounted to a wall or to a television mount itself. Soundbars are garbage, because the improvement in audio quality one gains from their installation is so extremely minimal, that it is practically non-existent. Certainly, the money one throws into a soundbar can just as easily be invested into a quality receiver and pair of speakers, or even just a simple pair of powered speakers, if one was not interested in the quality of audio a receiver introduces into their home television experience, and that is to say nothing of the fact that it can very easily (depending on the type) put one in a place where upgrading down the road to incorporate even better, and more immersive audio is a definite possibility. But even a quality pair of powered speakers can be had for well under the price of any "decent" soundbar, and the powered speakers would actually provide one with a noticeable improvement in audio quality. Keep in mind I did not say "volume", but "quality". Do not confuse the two, for a loud device does not a quality audio source make! Anyway, no offense meant to anyone who has spent good money on a soundbar in the past. (however, you know better now, so there will be no excuse come future endeavors for home audio needs...and lastly..... you should unmount or otherwise disassemble whatever attaches your soundbar to your television now, this very instant, before it corrupts your ears any longer, and run to your nearest home electronics store and ask them to help you find quality audio for your television - and if they try to sell you a soundbar, slap them and tell them you said "quality audio"!) I would just rip the soundbar off the television and throw it through the nearest window, and in doing so, would place myself in no better a situation than the other possibility in choice A. So, after not too much further thinking, it dawned on me that I had a pair of 8 inch studio monitors sitting in another room doing absolutely nothing, and so I checked them out and found that I would need an adapter which could take me from one 3.5mm audio connection (female) to two female XLR connections, one for each of the monitors. Because, I could've just used one, and it would've been a vast improvement over the previous mode of audio playback, but what type of person would use ONE studio monitor if there were two readily available?! Sorry to anyone who may be sporting one studio monitor, I......... Aww hell, I'm finished! I'm gonna go before I manage to make the entire community of Amazon customers angry with me!

Buy this cable though!!! It promotes QUALITY AUDIO, and that is a thing we can ALL be happy about!

top_five_star_obscene.text:
I have had over half a dozen different acoustics. Through out the years of have lost my interest in playing and sold them one by one. Recently I started to want to play again but didn't have a lot of cash to spend. I came across the Rogue RA-090 and thought it seemed like a good guitar and had great reviews. Well I'm giving it one more because this guitar has made me fall in love with playing again.

For the price you just can't not buy a better guitar. It plays and sounds beautiful! The action is low and makes playing very easy. Mine has a nice bright beautiful sound that I just can't hear enough of. I usually don't like bright sounding acoustics and prefer a deep sound but this one I love. The guitar seems rock solid and after playing it every day for hours a day for the past 2 weeks it still sounds just as good as the day I pulled it out of the box. I just can't say enough good things about this guitar. If you are looking for a guitar to learn on this is great for beginners because of the low action plus you get a great sounding guitar. It's a great guitar and you would have to be an idiot not to buy one especially for the price.

The only problem I have with mine is on the 6th(E) string on the 12th and 13th fret I get some string buzz. I'm pretty sure that I can fix it by adjusting the truss rod but at the moment I see no point in it as I hardly ever play in that area. Also mine does have a couple of frets that are a little sharp and could be filed down but they are barely noticeable. I will get around to fixing them in time. I still recommend this guitar to everybody. I have had guitars that cost 4 times the price that sounded like crap and played even worse than crap.

Just buy one and if you don't like it then I'll take it off your hands :)
top_five_star_obscene.title: Best guitar for the price PERIOD!

top_five_star_obscene.text: Ok, I'm a 46yo pro level drummer. I've done the tour thing, I've done the instructor thing, the drum tech for the big guys thing, the session drummer gigs, the gun for hire, the "hey I'm Michael, nice to meet you. Gotta song list for the first set? Let's go!" thing, all of the above, and more. I've had 2 dozen kits at least, from $500 beginner kits, to $9,000 shell packs. I've never had an acrylic kit though. I'm intrigued. This is a lot for the money. I see old style Tama bass drum spurs, Pearl style double tube lugs from masters series, DW style tom mounts, on stands, not the bass drum "which I can imagine as an even better thing on this shell material" and I see road series pro grade stands. The hi hat, and kick pedal maybe not as much, but they're certainly not garbage. I'm impressed, very much so. The recorded out of box video showcasing sound, didn't do much to help these out, with that horrible floor tom "whoam" but that's not the drum's fault really, more the heads. Speaking of, they seem to be a KO pinstripe. That's a 2 ply head, and if that's what these are, they'll get you up and going, and can be made to sound pretty good. I'd recommend new ones asap. That's an industry standard really. The only times I've not left a store with heads in hand, along with my new kit, is when I've been fortunate enough to buy custom drums "I've had a Roy era Ayotte kit, a DW Collector's kit, and a set of Ludwig Super Classic Maples I designed with Tama hardware, tall order lol" so the fact that these come with better than average crap stock heads, is a compliment. I think I want these. I think I'll get them someday. I'm not doing much these days with music, just playing an e kit into superior drummer 3, formulating a video content production that'll get me some pay hopefully, but I do still have my acoustic kits. I'd be interested in converting these over to be my new ekit, as I bet that'd be a killer look, with the electronic cymbal pads, and cables everywhere. Real industrial cyberpunk lookin. Anyhoo. If Donner wants, send me a kit, I'll do a video of the conversion, and promote the crap out of these on YouTube, and wherever else I can. I just felt compelled to give these a kick off review here, from a veteran players point of view. If you're new to drumming, firstly you don't know how lucky you are to have all these modern tools from the onset of your career, that us old folk didn't have. Second, this is a GREAT GREAT GREAT entry level to regional semipro level "dry ramen in the floorboard of a van hauling a uhaul trailer, baths in bathrooms rockstar dream baby!!" starter kit, that you'll get the first several years of your career out of very nicely. These are really nice, a helluva lot nicer than anything I had in my first 10 years or so, and a lot more than you'll get from bigger names entry offerings. That acrylic look, makes these drums appear to be 5x more expensive than they are, and scream "pro level drums" all day. You're welcome Donner, I just like giving credit where it's due, and that's here for sure.
top_five_star_obscene.title: There's a lot to unpack here, figuratively and literally

top_five_star_obscene.text:
As cheap as these strings were, I am pleasantly surprised with the results achieved.
I picked up an old, cheap electric guitar that needed some work. It had been dropped on it's head.
After repairing and playing it, it still sounded like crap so I had these strings, they were cheap and I put them on. Only took about 5 minutes to restring and was well worth the effort.
Okay, the guitar (and I am too embarrassed to give the brand) isn't my Gibson Les Paul or my Fender American Strat, but damn, it sounds good now. If I didn't have so many guitars, I might keep this one but I will probably sell it to make room for another.
These were well worth the few dollars I spent on them. Honestly, I won't restring my Fender with them, I'll use the Fender 250 bullets. But these are great for about any electric. With guitars, crappy strings kill it. Great strings can make a bad guitar okay, not great.
I will continue buying and using these on my lower end guitars but will spend the few extra bucks on my high end guitars.
